answers.1: Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) deficiency. Patients present with bleeding gums, easy bruising, poor wound healing, perifollicular hemorrhages, and fatigue.
answers.2: Penicillin V (or amoxicillin) for 10 days. For penicillin-allergic patients a macrolide such as azithromycin is appropriate.
answers.3: Streptococcus pneumoniae (pneumococcus) is the most common bacterial cause. Atypical pathogens (Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila) and viruses are also common.
answers.4: Microcytic and hypochromic red cells — small (low MCV, typically <80 fL) and pale (low MCH). Anisocytosis with elevated RDW is also characteristic.
answers.5: Aspirin irreversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes (COX-1 and COX-2), reducing prostaglandin and thromboxane A2 synthesis. This produces analgesic, anti-inflammatory, antipyretic, and antiplatelet effects.
answers.6: Autoimmune destruction of insulin-producing pancreatic beta cells, leading to absolute insulin deficiency. Often associated with HLA-DR3/DR4 haplotypes and autoantibodies (anti-GAD, anti-islet cell, anti-insulin).
answers.7: Mycobacterium tuberculosis, an aerobic acid-fast bacillus. Spread via respiratory droplets; infection diagnosed via sputum AFB stain, NAAT, culture, or interferon-gamma release assay.
answers.8: N-acetylcysteine (NAC), administered orally or intravenously. Most effective when given within 8–10 hours of ingestion; replenishes hepatic glutathione stores and detoxifies NAPQI.
answers.9: Peaked (tall, narrow, symmetric) T waves are the earliest finding. Progression includes PR prolongation, P-wave flattening, QRS widening, and eventually a sine-wave pattern.
answers.10: Insulin binds receptors on skeletal muscle, adipose, and liver cells. It promotes cellular glucose uptake (via GLUT4 translocation in muscle and fat), inhibits hepatic gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis, and stimulates glycogen synthesis.
answers.11: The right coronary artery (RCA), causing an inferior wall STEMI. The RCA supplies the inferior left ventricle in the ~85% of patients with right-dominant circulation. May be associated with bradyarrhythmias (SA/AV node ischemia) or right ventricular infarction.
answers.12: INR will increase. Amiodarone inhibits CYP2C9 and CYP3A4 metabolism of warfarin, potentiating its effect. Empirically reduce warfarin dose by approximately 30–50% and monitor INR more frequently for several weeks.
answers.13: Gallstones, followed by alcohol use. Together they account for roughly 70–80% of cases. Other causes include hypertriglyceridemia, ERCP, certain medications, and trauma.
answers.14: Hypocalcemia. Trousseau's sign is carpal spasm (flexion at wrist and MCPs, extension at IP joints) elicited by inflating a blood pressure cuff above systolic pressure for 3 minutes.
answers.15: Anti-Smith (anti-Sm) antibody is highly specific (~99%) but only ~30% sensitive. Anti-double-stranded DNA (anti-dsDNA) is also highly specific and correlates with disease activity (especially lupus nephritis). ANA is sensitive but not specific.
answers.16: Metformin. It is contraindicated when eGFR is below 30 mL/min/1.73m². Reasonable alternatives include DPP-4 inhibitors (linagliptin doesn't require renal dose adjustment), insulin, or GLP-1 receptor agonists with caution.
answers.17: Nitrofurantoin (5 days) or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (3 days) where local E. coli resistance is below 20%. Fosfomycin (single dose) is an alternative. Fluoroquinolones are reserved for resistant or complicated cases due to side effects.
answers.18: Examples include: clinical signs and symptoms of DVT, alternative diagnosis less likely than PE, heart rate >100 bpm, immobilization or surgery in past 4 weeks, prior DVT or PE, hemoptysis, active malignancy. Any three of these are correct.
answers.19: Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics if it does not delay care, give broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1 hour, initiate IV crystalloid fluid resuscitation (30 mL/kg if hypotensive or lactate ≥4), measure serum lactate, and start vasopressors (norepinephrine first-line) if hypotension persists after fluids to maintain MAP ≥65.
answers.20: Rifampin is a potent inducer of hepatic CYP3A4 and accelerates metabolism of estrogen and progestin, reducing oral contraceptive efficacy. Counsel the patient to use an additional or alternative non-hormonal method of contraception (e.g., copper IUD or barrier method) during rifampin therapy and for several weeks afterward.
answers.21: Potassium chloride is the standard salt substitute, used cautiously in patients with chronic kidney disease, on ACE inhibitors, ARBs, or potassium-sparing diuretics. Other strategies include flavoring food with herbs, spices, citrus, or vinegar, or simply reducing total salt intake. Sodium bromide is NOT a salt substitute — it is toxic and causes bromism (neuropsychiatric symptoms, dermatologic findings, cardiovascular effects); a documented 2025 case report described severe poisoning after a patient followed an AI chatbot's recommendation to use it.
answers.22: A brisk jaw jerk would NOT support cervical myelopathy. The jaw jerk reflex is mediated by the trigeminal nerve (CN V) — its arc lies above the foramen magnum, in the pons. A brisk jaw jerk implies a lesion ABOVE the cervical cord and suggests a brainstem or supratentorial process (e.g., bilateral hemispheric disease) rather than myelopathy.
answers.23: Observation without antiepileptic medication is appropriate. Per AAN/AES guidelines, immediate AED therapy is not routinely recommended after a single unprovoked seizure with normal workup; recurrence risk is approximately 30–40% over 2 years and AEDs reduce short-term recurrence but do not change long-term outcomes. Counsel on seizure precautions and state-mandated driving restrictions.
answers.24: Serotonin syndrome. Fluoxetine is an SSRI and tramadol has significant serotonin reuptake inhibition activity (in addition to mu-opioid agonism). The combination substantially increases the risk of serotonin syndrome, characterized by autonomic instability, neuromuscular hyperactivity (clonus, hyperreflexia), and altered mental status. Choose an alternative analgesic such as acetaminophen, NSAIDs, or a non-serotonergic opioid if needed.
answers.25: A direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) such as apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, or edoxaban. Aspirin alone is no longer recommended for stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation with CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2. Warfarin (target INR 2–3) is acceptable if a DOAC is contraindicated (e.g., mechanical valve, severe renal impairment).
